Avoiding local displacement, using place-based policy and pursuing resident-centred growth prevent aid-funded infrastructure from improving statistics while harming neighbours. integration outcome indicators and a dignity-centred approach reveal whether humanitarian support expands voice as well as immediate safety. Humanitarian aid responds to immediate crisis. Dispute settlement reduces unilateral retaliation. Persuasive design can support useful decisions or exploit weakness. Advertising standards require paid content to be identifiable.

3 · 3. Choice architecture and the problem of manipulation

Advertising influences people through language and imagery, but digital commerce also shapes behaviour through design. A shopping site can preselect options, display a countdown clock, hide cancellation controls or reveal extra fees only at the final stage. These techniques are commonly described as dark patterns because they exploit predictable weaknesses in attention and decision-making.

Some design choices are legitimate forms of simplification. Default settings can make complex services easier to use, and reminders may prevent people from abandoning a useful transaction. The ethical boundary is crossed when the interface benefits from confusion. Subscription traps, drip pricing and excessive urgency can reduce consumer autonomy by making the easiest action different from the action a fully informed user would choose.

A fair system should support meaningful consumer consent and enable consumers to reverse decisions without unreasonable friction. This requires advertising-regulation scrutiny, but rules should be carefully targeted. Banning every persuasive feature would be impossible and undesirable. The more defensible objective is to prevent deception, ensure that total prices are visible and require cancellation to be as straightforward as registration.

Aid should pursue equitable access for essential workers and underserved households. lifelong learning, transferable skills, targeted support and intergenerational mobility should guide whether a scholarship or school programme is genuinely inclusive. Poverty is experienced through daily conditions: unsafe drinking water, chronic stress, weak mental wellbeing and insecure livelihoods. Assistance must respond to individual circumstances while meeting a defensible evidence threshold. Digital targeting requires algorithmic transparency because households face information asymmetry. Aid registries should apply data minimisation for a legitimate purpose. People in entry-level roles need employers to provide paid training and share productivity gains as systems modernise. mission-driven research, replication studies and open knowledge spillovers help governments distinguish a portable lesson from a one-off success. climate monitoring, weather forecasting and coordinated disaster response then help direct scarce relief where the evidence shows the greatest need. Climate aid should connect climate adaptation with adaptation finance, flood resilience and early-warning systems. Support for soil biodiversity, nature-positive development and the reversal of pollinator decline can protect income without creating permanent grant dependence. Lower market concentration, more resilient supply chains, less food waste and careful management of water scarcity can make hunger prevention durable. Urban poverty combines housing insecurity with a difficult land-use trade-off. A circular economy can create repair livelihoods while lowering the material footprint. Development finance interacts with global value-chains, trade diversification and services trade. Avoiding local displacement, using place-based policy and pursuing resident-centred growth prevent aid-funded infrastructure from improving statistics while harming neighbours. Finally, civic participation and institutional coordination should include displaced people and receiving communities. Joint aid accountability requires open budgets and accessible complaints. Institutional legitimacy depends on fairness and results. Every step of the conversion funnel is tested for commercial effectiveness. Behavioural influence becomes problematic when it is hidden.

4 · 4. Consumer culture, identity and environmental pressure

Advertising does more than sell individual objects. Through aspirational branding and lifestyle marketing, it connects products with attractiveness, competence, creativity or social belonging. A watch can become a sign of seriousness, a phone a symbol of modernity and a particular coffee a marker of taste. In this environment, status consumption may feel less like vanity than participation in a shared culture.

The psychological effect is ambiguous. Consumption can provide pleasure, convenience and self-expression. However, constant comparison may turn optional goods into perceived necessities. Social platforms intensify conspicuous consumption because purchases are displayed to an audience and measured through reactions. The result can be social pressure, impulse buying and, in more serious cases, household debt.

There is also an environmental cost. Rapid product cycles encourage replacement before objects have reached the end of their useful life, while packaging, delivery and returns add further waste. A culture organised around permanent novelty may therefore increase economic activity without improving material wellbeing. Consumers have responsibility, but firms and platforms also shape the pace and visibility of demand.

evidence-based policymaking, honest cost-benefit analysis and long-term public value matter more than a donor's preferred launch date. lifelong learning, transferable skills, targeted support and intergenerational mobility should guide whether a scholarship or school programme is genuinely inclusive. secure employment and fewer structural barriers therefore belong inside development evaluation. legal safeguards, fewer employment barriers and transparent decisions protect public confidence in both local and donor institutions. regulatory oversight, procedural fairness and freedom of expression protect people who contest an exclusion decision. independent oversight can close an accountability gap, while agencies build up public data systems instead of exporting sensitive records indefinitely. People in entry-level roles need employers to provide paid training and share productivity gains as systems modernise. mission-driven research, replication studies and open knowledge spillovers help governments distinguish a portable lesson from a one-off success. Earth observation and satellite data can identify damaged roads and crops. Climate aid should connect climate adaptation with adaptation finance, flood resilience and early-warning systems. Support for soil biodiversity, nature-positive development and the reversal of pollinator decline can protect income without creating permanent grant dependence. Lower market concentration, more resilient supply chains, less food waste and careful management of water scarcity can make hunger prevention durable. Strong municipal delivery capacity supports sustainable urban development instead of letting short projects add to fragmented infrastructure. Better resource productivity also reduces economic externalities and narrows the water-security gap affecting low-income settlements. A shared trade benefit requires donor policy to acknowledge the adjustment burden carried by workers and small producers. Projects need community consent and careful attention to resident sentiment. integration outcome indicators and a dignity-centred approach reveal whether humanitarian support expands voice as well as immediate safety. Local ownership improves relevance and sustainability. National sovereignty remains central to international law. Trend simulation can make a campaign appear organically popular. Advertising policy must consider long-term consequences for children.

5 · 5. What balanced regulation should try to achieve

The strongest response is neither to celebrate advertising as pure information nor to condemn it as universal manipulation. Advertising can help new businesses reach customers, finance journalism and introduce people to useful products. It can support market competition by allowing smaller firms to challenge established brands. The question is which practices preserve useful information and which undermine informed choice.

A programme of balanced regulation should focus on transparency, vulnerable groups and demonstrable harm. Paid endorsements should be clearly labelled, shopper data should not be collected through obscure consent systems, and vulnerable consumers—especially children—should receive stronger protection. At the same time, regulators should avoid rules so broad that they silence ordinary recommendations or prevent legitimate businesses from communicating.

Education remains essential. commercial media literacy and digital literacy cannot eliminate commercial pressure, but they can make citizens less passive. Schools can teach how social proof, scarcity messages and recommendation algorithms work. Firms, meanwhile, should practise ethical marketing and accept corporate accountability when claims are misleading. In the long run, honest persuasion may prove worthwhile through stronger loyalty, while deceptive tactics create only temporary advantage and lasting distrust.

evidence-based policymaking, honest cost-benefit analysis and long-term public value matter more than a donor's preferred launch date. lifelong learning, transferable skills, targeted support and intergenerational mobility should guide whether a scholarship or school programme is genuinely inclusive. secure employment and fewer structural barriers therefore belong inside development evaluation. legal safeguards, fewer employment barriers and transparent decisions protect public confidence in both local and donor institutions. regulatory oversight, procedural fairness and freedom of expression protect people who contest an exclusion decision. Aid registries should apply data minimisation for a legitimate purpose. Donor-funded automation should support worker augmentation, not silent job displacement. Development learning depends on funding continuity and scientific independence. climate monitoring, weather forecasting and coordinated disaster response then help direct scarce relief where the evidence shows the greatest need. Even managed retreat requires finance that protects agency and livelihoods rather than merely moving risk elsewhere. Support for soil biodiversity, nature-positive development and the reversal of pollinator decline can protect income without creating permanent grant dependence. Lower market concentration, more resilient supply chains, less food waste and careful management of water scarcity can make hunger prevention durable. Strong municipal delivery capacity supports sustainable urban development instead of letting short projects add to fragmented infrastructure. Better resource productivity also reduces economic externalities and narrows the water-security gap affecting low-income settlements. Development finance interacts with global value-chains, trade diversification and services trade. Avoiding local displacement, using place-based policy and pursuing resident-centred growth prevent aid-funded infrastructure from improving statistics while harming neighbours. Finally, civic participation and institutional coordination should include displaced people and receiving communities. Sustainable financing reduces programme collapse. Treaty obligations require domestic implementation. Product placement can influence viewers without interrupting the story.

Continue to model essays

Idea-building model

5. Advanced C2 essay

Question: Some people believe advertising is beneficial because it informs consumers and supports businesses, while others think it manipulates people and encourages unnecessary consumption. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.
Extended model · 1410 words · designed to build arguments, not imitate exam length

Advertising is one of the most visible institutions of a market economy, yet its social role remains deeply contested. To its defenders, it is a mechanism through which firms communicate information, finance media and compete for public attention. To its critics, it is a sophisticated system for manufacturing dissatisfaction and converting personal insecurity into demand. Both descriptions are partly accurate. Advertising can reduce information costs and widen consumer choice, but the modern advertising ecosystem has moved far beyond the simple announcement of price and availability. My view is therefore qualified: advertising is economically useful and culturally creative when its commercial intent is transparent, but data-intensive and psychologically manipulative practices require firmer regulation.

The strongest argument in favour of advertising is that markets depend on communication. A new company may produce a genuinely better service, yet consumers cannot choose it if they do not know it exists. Advertising can therefore support market competition by allowing entrants to challenge firms whose advantage rests mainly on familiarity. This is especially important in crowded markets, where brand recall influences purchasing even when products are technically similar. Informative campaigns can explain prices, functions and differences, reducing the time consumers spend searching. Were all promotion prohibited, established companies with large distribution networks would often become even more dominant, because reputation itself would act as an inherited advertising asset.

Advertising also finances a substantial part of the public information environment. Newspapers, podcasts, search engines, entertainment platforms and independent creators frequently provide content at little or no direct cost because advertisers subsidise production. This arrangement is imperfect, but it has broadened access to information and culture. In addition, the history of advertising demonstrates that commercial communication can involve genuine artistic skill. Memorable campaigns use humour, music and brand storytelling to become part of cultural memory. Not every advert is an insult to intelligence; some are compact works of narrative design that audiences actively enjoy.

Nevertheless, the case for advertising weakens when persuasion is presented as neutral information. The contemporary commercial attention market does not merely display messages. It observes behaviour, predicts vulnerability and places promotion at moments calculated to maximise response. algorithmic targeting can be convenient when it removes irrelevant adverts, yet it also allows firms to address different consumers with different emotional triggers. A person anxious about appearance, status or social acceptance may receive a sequence of messages that amplify precisely those concerns. The commercial system thus does not simply answer existing demand; it can intensify dissatisfaction until a purchase appears to offer psychological relief.

influencer marketing illustrates the problem particularly clearly. An influencer may appear to be sharing an ordinary routine, reviewing a favourite product or offering personal advice. However, affiliate links, sponsorship agreements and platform incentives can quietly shape what is recommended. Because followers have developed parasocial trust, the recommendation may bypass the scepticism normally applied to a conventional advert. Not only is the message embedded within entertainment, but the apparent speaker is also treated as a familiar individual rather than a paid representative. Clear disclosure labels are therefore essential. Without them, audiences cannot evaluate commercial intent fairly.

Digital interfaces can also manipulate after the advert has attracted attention. default settings, scarcity tactics, subscription traps and drip pricing influence the architecture of the decision itself. A consumer may be told that only two items remain, discover unavoidable fees at the final stage or find that cancelling a trial requires far more effort than starting it. Such dark patterns undermine consumer autonomy because they profit from confusion rather than preference. The distinction matters. All communication influences behaviour to some degree, but ethical persuasion leaves the person able to understand, compare and reject the offer without unreasonable friction.

A further criticism concerns the broader culture generated by continual promotion. aspirational branding links products with idealised identities, while social media makes conspicuous consumption visible and measurable. People are encouraged to regard clothing, devices, travel and even domestic interiors as public evidence of competence or belonging. This can produce pleasure and creativity, but it can also create social pressure and impulse buying. For households already under financial strain, small repeated purchases may accumulate into serious debt. More consumption does not automatically produce greater material wellbeing, especially when desire is continuously renewed before satisfaction has time to settle.

environmental costs strengthen the case for restraint. Advertising often supports business models based on rapid replacement, seasonal novelty and high return rates. Products that remain functional are redefined as outdated, while fast delivery separates the moment of desire from any period of reflection. The resulting supply chains require extraction, manufacturing, packaging and transport. It would be simplistic to blame advertising alone for environmental damage, because consumers, producers and public policy all contribute. Even so, promotion accelerates the cycle by making novelty culturally valuable and delay psychologically uncomfortable.

The appropriate response is not a general ban. Such a measure would restrict legitimate speech, entrench established brands and remove funding from media and cultural production. Instead, governments should pursue balanced regulation. sponsored content must be clearly identified; total prices should be visible before payment; cancellation should be straightforward; and shopper data should not be used for targeted promotion without meaningful consent. Stronger rules are justified for children and other vulnerable consumers because they may be less able to recognise persuasive intent. Regulators should also require evidence for health, environmental and financial claims, where misleading information can cause concrete harm.

Education must complement regulation. commercial media literacy should teach not only how to analyse slogans and images but also how recommendation systems, engagement metrics and social proof influence exposure. Consumers who understand that popularity can be manufactured are less likely to confuse synthetic virality with collective judgement. Schools cannot make young people immune to persuasion, nor should they attempt to. They can, however, provide a vocabulary for recognising techniques and create a pause between emotional response and purchase.

It is also necessary to distinguish among types of advertising rather than treating the industry as a single practice. A poster announcing a local theatre production, a supermarket price comparison and a personalised video designed from intimate purchase-behaviour data all seek attention, but they do not create the same risks. evidence-led advertising policy should focus on the mechanism of harm. A truthful price advert may improve consumer choice, whereas a campaign that conceals paid relationships or targets addiction-related vulnerability may require intervention. This distinction protects both consumers and legitimate commercial speech.

Corporate incentives also deserve attention. Firms often defend questionable techniques by claiming that competitors use them, making unilateral restraint commercially difficult. advertising-regulation scrutiny can therefore improve the market rather than merely constrain it. If every company must disclose sponsorship, show total prices and offer simple cancellation, honest firms are no longer disadvantaged by competitors willing to exploit confusion. ethical marketing then becomes compatible with profitability rather than an act of corporate self-sacrifice.

Finally, consumers themselves should not be described as passive victims. People interpret advertising creatively, ignore much of it and sometimes use brands for purposes never intended by marketers. Consumer backlash can force companies to withdraw offensive campaigns, while online communities expose publicly misleading claims with remarkable speed. Nonetheless, individual resistance is uneven. A system should not assume that every person has unlimited time, technical knowledge and emotional distance. Consumer autonomy is best protected when personal responsibility operates within fair commercial rules.

A final issue is the financial relationship between advertising and public communication. Advertising-funded media can widen access, but dependence on commercial revenue may also shape editorial priorities. Content that attracts predictable audiences and strong engagement can become more valuable than slow, specialised or socially important reporting. This does not mean that advertising-funded media are inherently untrustworthy. It means that the subsidy has conditions. Public-interest journalism, educational content and minority cultural production may need alternative funding models when commercial attention is insufficient. A diverse media system should therefore include advertising, subscriptions and carefully protected public support rather than relying on one source alone.

In conclusion, advertising performs useful economic and cultural functions, but its legitimacy depends on the conditions under which persuasion occurs. Informative and creative campaigns can support competition, fund content and help consumers discover valuable products. By contrast, hidden sponsorship, surveillance advertising and manipulative interface design weaken informed choice. The strongest policy is therefore not to eliminate advertising but to insist on commercial transparency, consumer autonomy and corporate accountability. When persuasion respects those principles, it can remain part of a healthy market; when it exploits confusion or vulnerability, public regulation is justified.

Exam-length model

6. Realistic IELTS essay · approximately 300 words

Question: Some people believe advertising is beneficial because it informs consumers and supports businesses, while others think it manipulates people and encourages unnecessary consumption. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.
Model answer · 315 words

Advertising is often criticised for encouraging people to buy things they do not need, although it also gives consumers information and helps businesses compete. In my view, advertising is useful in principle, but modern digital promotion requires stronger rules because commercial messages are increasingly personalised and difficult to recognise.

On the positive side, advertising informs the public about new products, prices and services. This is particularly important for smaller companies that need to attract customers away from established brands. Advertising also finances newspapers, online videos and other forms of content that people can access cheaply or free of charge. Some campaigns are creative and memorable, so advertising can contribute to popular culture as well as economic activity.

However, promotion becomes harmful when it exploits insecurity or hides its commercial purpose. Influencers may present paid recommendations as personal advice, while targeted adverts use purchase-behaviour data to reach consumers at vulnerable moments. In addition, scarcity messages and one-click payment systems encourage impulse buying. This can create unnecessary spending and may contribute to household debt, especially among younger consumers who are exposed to advertising throughout the day.

For this reason, governments should require clear disclosure labels, protect shopper data and restrict misleading claims. Advertising aimed at children should face particularly strict standards. At the same time, a complete ban would be unrealistic and would reduce competition. commercial media literacy education can help consumers understand persuasive techniques and compare products more carefully. Smaller firms still need affordable access to audiences, but this benefit depends on transparent comparison rather than hidden influence. Clear sponsorship, complete prices and genuine control over profiling preserve useful communication while limiting manipulation.

In conclusion, advertising provides real benefits by sharing information and supporting business, but it should not be allowed to manipulate people through hidden sponsorship or deceptive design. Transparent advertising should remain legal, while practices that undermine informed choice should be regulated more firmly.

Why the exam-length essay is strong

Direct position

The introduction answers the task and preserves a clear line of argument.

Causal explanation

Each body paragraph explains a mechanism rather than listing opinions.

Developed contrast

Competing benefits and risks are weighed under realistic conditions.

Policy mechanism

Concrete safeguards turn principle into implementable policy.

Recycled language

Earlier collocations return as part of the reasoning rather than as decoration.

Controlled complexity

Advanced grammar remains clear enough for realistic exam conditions.

7. Advanced grammar transformations

1. If governments ignored hidden advertising, public trust would decline. (conditional inversion)

Were governments to ignore hidden advertising, public trust would decline.

2. Personalised advertising affects choice. It also affects attention. (not only... but also)

Not only does personalised advertising affect choice, but it also shapes attention.

3. Consumers need transparent prices, not more promotional messages. (cleft sentence)

What consumers need is transparent pricing, not more promotional messages.

4. Platforms collect behavioural data and then predict purchases. (participle clause)

Collecting purchase-behaviour data, platforms can predict likely purchases.

5. Advertising can support competition, but it can also manipulate vulnerable users. (although)

Although advertising can support competition, it can also manipulate vulnerable users.

6. Influencer marketing expanded, and disclosure became more difficult. (nominalisation)

The expansion of influencer marketing made disclosure more difficult.

7. Platforms changed the boundary between content and promotion. The effect continues. (present perfect)

Platforms have changed the boundary between content and promotion.

8. Regulators did not act earlier, so consumers remain exposed now. (mixed conditional)

If regulators had acted earlier, consumers would be less exposed now.

9. Advertising exploits insecurity, so it can increase unnecessary demand. (participle clause)

Exploiting insecurity, advertising can increase unnecessary demand.

10. Although a disclosure label is useful, it does not prove a claim. (fronted concession)

Useful though a disclosure label is, it does not prove a claim.

11. Regulators did not examine the interface, so they missed the dark pattern. (third conditional)

Had regulators examined the interface, they would not have missed the dark pattern.

12. Platforms collect shopper data and shape the order of recommendations. (not only...but also)

Not only do platforms collect shopper data, but they also shape the order of recommendations.

13. People have faced embedded sponsorship for years. (present-perfect continuous)

Consumers have been facing embedded sponsorship for years.

14. Because children cannot reliably identify persuasive intent, they need stronger protection. (nominalisation)

Children's limited recognition of persuasive intent justifies stronger protection.

15. The price looked temporary, but the subscription renewed indefinitely. (whereas)

The price appeared temporary, whereas the subscription renewed indefinitely.

16. The firm claimed the product was scarce. The claim was fabricated. (relative clause)

The scarcity claim, which the firm had fabricated, created artificial urgency.

17. If targeting were contextual, it would collect less data. (mixed emphasis)

What contextual targeting would reduce is the collection of shopper data.

18. Regulators should assess both the claim and the way it is presented. (correlative structure)

Regulators should assess not only the claim itself but also the way in which it is presented.

8. Native Academic Toolbox

1. Upgrade: I think clear labels are important because people need to know when a post is paid.

The primary justification for clear disclosure labels is that audiences must recognise when a post is commercially funded.

2. Upgrade: People forget that personalised advertising depends on tracking.

A frequently overlooked consequence of personalised advertising is its dependence on extensive behavioural tracking.

3. Upgrade: This is not simply useful advertising versus harmful advertising.

The issue should not be viewed as a simple choice between beneficial and harmful advertising.

4. Upgrade: We should accept that some advertising helps small businesses.

A more nuanced position would recognise that advertising can help smaller firms reach consumers.

5. Upgrade: If regulators ignore dark patterns, consumers will lose control.

Were regulators to ignore dark patterns, consumer autonomy would continue to weaken.

6. Upgrade: Clear prices help consumers and also improve competition.

Not only do transparent prices protect consumers, but they also support fair market competition.

7. Upgrade: In reality, the priority should be stopping hidden sponsorship.

From a practical standpoint, the priority should be preventing hidden commercial relationships.

8. Upgrade: Over time, honest advertising may create more loyalty.

In the long run, ethical marketing is likely to strengthen customer loyalty.

9. Upgrade: There is a conflict between relevance and privacy.

This creates a policy trade-off between advertising relevance and personal privacy.

10. Upgrade: We should not ban all advertising; we should regulate deception.

The strongest solution is not to eliminate advertising, but to regulate deceptive and manipulative practices.

11. Upgrade: Advertising tricks people.

Manipulative design can undermine consumer autonomy by disguising a commercial choice as a neutral default.

12. Upgrade: Children see too many adverts.

Children's repeated media exposure creates a strong case for age-sensitive limits on frequency and targeting.

13. Upgrade: Influencers should be honest.

Influencers should disclose sponsored content prominently and substantiate factual product claims.

14. Upgrade: Personalised ads can be useful but invasive.

Personalisation creates a tension between relevance and meaningful consumer consent.

15. Upgrade: People buy too much online.

Frictionless checkout and scarcity tactics can intensify avoidable consumption and financial stress.

9. IELTS Speaking

Part 1 · 15 questions

PART 1 · 1

Do you often notice advertising in your daily life?

Suggested phrasal verbs
scroll past
Model answer · 80 words

I notice advertising constantly, especially on my phone, public transport and shopping streets. Most conventional social media ads barely register because I simply scroll past them, but sponsored content is harder to recognise immediately. Repeated media exposure also creates ad fatigue, so I pay attention mainly when a message is genuinely useful, clearly disclosed or unusually creative. The volume makes advertising feel less like an occasional interruption and more like part of the environment through which I make everyday choices.

PART 1 · 2

What kind of advertising do you remember most clearly?

Suggested phrasal verbs
wear off
Model answer · 79 words

I usually remember advertising that tells a short story rather than listing product features. A strong emotional appeal or an unexpected piece of humour improves brand recall because the advert feels like entertainment. By contrast, the effect of repetitive banner ads tends to wear off very quickly, even when I see the same brand several times a day. A memorable campaign respects the audience enough to leave space for interpretation instead of repeating a slogan until familiarity replaces interest.

PART 1 · 3

Do you trust advertisements on social media?

Suggested phrasal verbs
be taken in by
Model answer · 88 words

I treat social media advertising cautiously. I may find a recommendation useful, but I do not automatically be taken in by the lifestyle presented by an influencer. sponsored content can look almost identical to an ordinary post, so I first check whether the commercial intent is clearly disclosed. Transparent advertising earns more public trust than a recommendation that hides the relationship with a brand. I also compare the recommendation with independent reviews, because a disclosure label identifies payment but does not prove that the claim itself is reliable.

PART 1 · 4

Have you ever bought something because of an advertisement?

Suggested phrasal verbs
give in to
Model answer · 84 words

Yes, targeted promotion has influenced me a few times, particularly when I was already considering a product. In those cases, the advert helped me compare options and make a purchase decision. I have also occasionally given in to impulse buying when a discount appeared urgent, although I try to wait before paying so that a temporary feeling does not become an unnecessary purchase. That experience taught me to separate useful product information from urgency created by a countdown, limited stock message or personalised discount.

PART 1 · 5

Do you prefer online advertising or traditional advertising?

Suggested phrasal verbs
scroll past
Model answer · 80 words

I prefer traditional advertising when it is creative because a good poster or television campaign can use brand storytelling without following me across different websites. personalised advertising is sometimes more relevant, but it can also feel intrusive. Online, I scroll past most messages automatically, whereas a well-designed public advert can be noticed without demanding shopper data. For me, relevance is valuable only when I understand why I was targeted and can refuse the tracking without losing access to the service.

PART 1 · 6

Are there too many advertisements where you live?

Suggested phrasal verbs
resist pressure from
Model answer · 80 words

There are probably too many, particularly around shopping centres and busy roads. market saturation makes each advertising campaign less noticeable and can make public space feel visually chaotic. People sometimes resist pressure from large digital screens because they dominate the streetscape. I do not object to all advertising, but I think cities should control scale, brightness and placement. The problem is not one poster but the cumulative claim on attention, especially when public space offers almost no quiet visual alternative.

PART 1 · 7

Do you think advertisements influence children strongly?

Suggested phrasal verbs
be taken in by
Model answer · 83 words

Children are a vulnerable target audience because they may not fully understand the difference between entertainment and promotion. They can easily be taken in by the idea that a product will make them popular or successful. advertising literacy should therefore begin at school, and companies should face stricter limits when they market food, games or expensive products directly to young people. Children may recognise a sales pitch in theory yet still struggle to resist characters, rewards and repetition designed around their developmental vulnerabilities.

PART 1 · 8

What do you usually do before buying an expensive product?

Suggested phrasal verbs
shop around
Model answer · 83 words

Before an expensive purchase, I shop around rather than trusting the first promotion I see. I compare prices, read several online reviews and check whether the product actually fits my needs. That process improves consumer choice because it separates useful information from urgency. I also wait a day or two, since an attractive advert can create enthusiasm that disappears quickly. I also wait at least a day, since time makes it easier to distinguish a durable need from excitement produced by persuasive design.

PART 1 · 9

Do you follow any influencers who recommend products?

Suggested phrasal verbs
click through
Model answer · 81 words

I follow a few creators who occasionally recommend books or technology, but I remain aware of influencer marketing. parasocial trust can make a creator seem like a friend, even though the relationship is one-sided. I may click through to examine a product, but I check for affiliate links and compare other opinions before deciding whether the recommendation is genuinely useful. I trust them more when they discuss limitations, refuse unsuitable sponsors and keep commercial posts visibly separate from ordinary personal updates.

PART 1 · 10

Is brand loyalty important to you?

Suggested phrasal verbs
accumulate over time
Model answer · 81 words

brand loyalty matters only when it has been built up through reliable quality or service. I do not stay with a company simply because its brand image is attractive. However, if a business consistently handles problems well, customer loyalty becomes rational because changing suppliers creates uncertainty. I still compare alternatives occasionally so that loyalty does not become automatic habit. A reliable history can reduce search time, but I still compare repairability, price and service because loyalty should not remove competitive pressure.

PART 1 · 11

Do people in your country enjoy shopping?

Suggested phrasal verbs
rein in
Model answer · 87 words

Shopping is a popular leisure activity in many cities, so shopping culture is quite visible. People meet in malls, browse online and sometimes use products as symbols of success. At the same time, rising prices have encouraged some households to rein in non-essential purchases. buying habits therefore reflect both social aspiration and practical financial pressure. At the same time, high living costs have made price comparison and second-hand platforms more visible, so enjoyment increasingly coexists with financial caution. Published evidence would make that judgement easier to defend.

PART 1 · 12

Have you ever ignored an advertisement and regretted it?

Suggested phrasal verbs
scroll past
Model answer · 80 words

Not often, although I have occasionally scrolled past a useful discount and later paid more. Even so, I would rather miss one promotional message than react to every offer. Constant attention would make sensible consumer choice impossible. For an important purchase, I prefer active price comparison instead of relying on whatever advertisement happens to appear in my feed. If an offer disappears, I usually assume another will appear; avoiding manufactured urgency protects me from far more regret than it causes.

PART 1 · 13

Would you like to work in advertising?

Suggested phrasal verbs
accumulate over time
Model answer · 80 words

The creative side would interest me because developing an advertising campaign requires concise writing, visual thinking and an understanding of audience segmentation. I would especially enjoy brand storytelling, where a team builds up a clear identity over time. However, I would be uncomfortable promoting products I considered harmful or using shopper data in ways consumers did not understand. I would prefer research or standards work, where creativity is combined with evidence about whether audiences understand the claim and the sponsorship.

PART 1 · 14

Do you think advertising can be artistic?

Suggested phrasal verbs
prove worthwhile
Model answer · 86 words

Yes, some advertising is clearly artistic. A short campaign can combine photography, music, humour and emotional appeal with remarkable precision. Strong brand storytelling may remain in cultural memory for decades, even among people who never bought the product. Creativity can also prove worthwhile commercially, although artistic quality does not excuse misleading claims or harmful promotion. Its commercial purpose does not erase craft, although artistic quality should never excuse a misleading price, hidden sponsor or exploitative target audience. Published evidence would make that judgement easier to defend.

PART 1 · 15

Will people see more or less advertising in the future?

Suggested phrasal verbs
withdraw from
Model answer · 81 words

People will probably see fewer obvious adverts but more personalised advertising integrated into ordinary content. In the commercial attention market, commercial messages increasingly appear as recommendations, entertainment or search results. At the same time, ad fatigue and privacy concerns may encourage users to withdraw from tracking or pay for subscription services. Advertising will not disappear; it will become less visibly separate. People may encounter fewer obvious banners but more recommendations embedded in entertainment, search results, virtual spaces and automated personal assistants.

Part 3 · 15 questions

PART 3 · 1

Why has influencer marketing become so effective?

Suggested phrasal verbs
be taken in byclick through
Model answer · 119 words

influencer marketing is effective because it combines commercial promotion with an existing social relationship. Followers may have watched a creator for years, so parasocial trust makes a recommendation feel personal rather than institutional. In addition, sponsored content is often presented in the same style as ordinary posts, which reduces the psychological resistance people show towards conventional adverts. Audiences may be taken in by the creator’s lifestyle before they consciously evaluate the product, and it is easy to click through immediately. The practice is not necessarily deceptive, but its power explains why disclosure must be clear and visible. The recommendation also arrives inside a familiar routine, so followers process it as social information before they consciously evaluate its commercial intent.

PART 3 · 2

Should governments restrict advertising aimed at children?

Suggested phrasal verbs
retire from saleresist pressure from
Model answer · 126 words

Yes, although the restrictions should focus on products and techniques that create clear risks. Children are vulnerable consumers because they have limited advertising literacy and may not understand persuasive intent. Governments could retire from sale behavioural targeting of minors and restrict campaigns for unhealthy food, gambling-like games or expensive products. Companies may resist pressure from such rules by arguing that parents are responsible, but parents cannot monitor every personalised message delivered through a device. public regulation should therefore create a safer baseline while education helps children become more critical as they grow older. Rules should cover games, influencer content and profiling as well as television, because the most persuasive formats are often the least recognisable as advertisements. Published evidence would make that judgement easier to defend.

PART 3 · 3

Is personalised advertising more useful or more intrusive?

Suggested phrasal verbs
withdraw from
Model answer · 126 words

It can be useful when it reduces irrelevant messages and helps consumers discover products they genuinely need. However, the usefulness depends on how purchase-behaviour data are collected and whether people understand the process. If tracking is extensive, hidden or difficult to refuse, personalised advertising becomes intrusive because consumer autonomy is weakened. Users should be able to withdraw from profiling without losing basic access to a service. In my view, relevance is a benefit, but it does not justify unlimited surveillance. Clear consent and limits on sensitive data are therefore essential. A fair system would minimise shopper data, offer meaningful consumer consent and let people withdraw from tracking without facing a deliberately worse service. Parliamentary or regulatory scrutiny would make the resulting obligation easier to defend publicly.

PART 3 · 4

How does advertising affect people’s sense of identity?

Suggested phrasal verbs
be taken in by
Model answer · 119 words

Advertising often connects products with identities rather than practical functions. aspirational branding suggests that a certain car, phone or style of clothing represents confidence, intelligence or belonging. People may then be taken in by a social image and use status consumption to communicate who they are. This can be harmless self-expression, but it also creates social pressure when ordinary possessions are treated as evidence of personal worth. The effect is strongest on platforms where purchases are publicly displayed. A healthier culture would allow people to enjoy products without making consumption the main language of identity. The pressure is strongest when social proof makes a commercial style appear to be the normal standard of success for an entire peer group.

PART 3 · 5

What are the main ethical problems with data-driven advertising?

Suggested phrasal verbs
withdraw fromexpose publicly
Model answer · 118 words

The first problem is opacity. Consumers rarely know how much information is collected, how consumer profiling works or which companies receive their data through data brokerage. The second issue is power: surveillance advertising can target people when they are emotionally or financially vulnerable. Finally, refusing tracking is often more difficult than accepting it. Users should be able to withdraw from data collection through a simple process, and regulators should expose publicly firms that hide consent choices. Data can make advertising more relevant, but relevance should not be purchased at the cost of privacy and meaningful consumer consent. Independent auditing should test not only privacy compliance but whether targeting systematically exploits debt, illness, age or moments of emotional vulnerability.

PART 3 · 6

Can advertising make markets more competitive?

Suggested phrasal verbs
accumulate over timeprove worthwhile
Model answer · 118 words

Yes, because advertising allows new firms to reach a target audience and challenge established companies whose main advantage is brand recall. A small business can accumulate over time recognition more quickly through digital campaigns than through physical expansion alone. Good communication may therefore prove worthwhile by helping consumers discover better or cheaper alternatives. However, the effect is not automatically competitive. Large firms can dominate attention through enormous budgets and exclusive data. Competition improves only when claims are truthful, platforms are reasonably open and smaller firms can access audiences without paying excessively high fees. Competition improves only when claims are comparable and truthful; otherwise the firm with the largest persuasion budget can obscure a better but less visible product.

PART 3 · 7

Why do people continue to make impulse purchases?

Suggested phrasal verbs
give in towear off
Model answer · 128 words

impulse buying occurs because commercial systems reduce the time available for reflection. scarcity tactics create urgency, while social proof suggests that many other people have already approved the product. One-click payment then removes practical friction. Consumers may give in to the feeling that they will lose an opportunity, even though the desire may wear off shortly afterwards. Emotional states also matter: boredom, stress and insecurity can make a purchase feel rewarding. The most effective response is not simply stronger willpower, but design that shows total costs clearly and allows a brief pause before payment. Cooling-off periods, spending alerts and friction before payment can restore reflection without assuming that consumers simply lack willpower or financial knowledge. Parliamentary or regulatory scrutiny would make the resulting obligation easier to defend publicly.

PART 3 · 8

Should influencers be responsible for products they promote?

Suggested phrasal verbs
cash in onexpose publicly
Model answer · 125 words

They should be responsible for claims they make and for disclosing paid relationships, although they cannot guarantee every aspect of a product. If an influencer chooses to cash in on audience trust, commercial transparency becomes part of the job. They should verify factual statements, avoid promoting obvious scams and state when links generate commission. Audiences and regulators are also justified in calling out misleading claims. However, legal responsibility should remain proportionate: a creator who reports an honest personal experience should not be treated like the manufacturer of a defective product. Responsibility should rise when they make factual claims, target vulnerable followers or repeatedly present sponsorship as personal experience rather than paid promotion. Parliamentary or regulatory scrutiny would make the resulting obligation easier to defend publicly.

PART 3 · 9

How can schools improve media literacy?

Suggested phrasal verbs
scroll pastshop around
Model answer · 125 words

Schools should move beyond asking students to identify slogans. commercial media literacy now requires understanding how feeds are selected, how synthetic virality can imitate genuine popularity and why sponsored content may resemble ordinary communication. Students could compare advertisements, examine disclosure labels and trace the steps from attention to purchase. They should also practise habits such as shopping around, checking sources and pausing before reacting. The aim is not to teach young people to scroll past every commercial message. It is to help them distinguish useful information, creative persuasion and manipulative design. Students should analyse real interfaces, trace who benefits from a recommendation and practise redesigning a manipulative choice into a transparent one. Parliamentary or regulatory scrutiny would make the resulting obligation easier to defend publicly.

PART 3 · 10

Does consumer culture reduce happiness?

Suggested phrasal verbs
rein in
Model answer · 125 words

It can reduce happiness when social status becomes tied to permanent consumption. shopping culture offers real pleasure and convenience, but conspicuous consumption encourages comparison, so satisfaction is quickly replaced by another standard to meet. material wellbeing depends on security, health and time as well as possessions. People who rein in unnecessary buying sometimes discover that they lose little practical value. Nevertheless, consumption is not inherently shallow. Products can support hobbies, comfort and identity. The problem is a culture in which buying becomes the default response to boredom, insecurity or the need for belonging. Wellbeing is more likely to decline when consumption becomes a measure of adequacy, leaving relationships, competence and security permanently compared with commercial ideals. Published evidence would make that judgement easier to defend.

PART 3 · 11

How should online shopping platforms be regulated?

Suggested phrasal verbs
enrol inwithdraw from
Model answer · 120 words

Regulation should focus on transparency and symmetry. Platforms should display the full price early, avoid drip pricing and make it as easy to cancel as it is to enrol in a service. dark patterns that hide choices or repeatedly pressure users should be restricted because they undermine consumer rights. At the same time, regulators should avoid prescribing every detail of interface design. The principle should be that consumers can understand the transaction, withdraw from additional services and reverse an accidental choice without unreasonable difficulty. Clear standards would also protect honest firms from competitors that profit through confusion. Regulators also need access to interface tests and recommendation systems, because harmful design may never appear in the final contract or privacy policy.

PART 3 · 12

Why do some advertising campaigns cause consumer backlash?

Suggested phrasal verbs
resist pressure fromexpose publicly
Model answer · 118 words

consumer backlash usually occurs when a campaign appears dishonest, insensitive or inconsistent with the company’s behaviour. A brand may present itself as environmentally responsible while maintaining wasteful practices, or use social causes mainly to attract attention. Audiences then resist pressure from the gap between the message and reality. Social media allows people to expose publicly contradictions quickly, so damage to brand image can spread rapidly. Companies often recover when they explain the mistake and change their conduct. Defensive responses are less effective because public trust depends on accountability rather than perfect public relations. Backlash becomes stronger when a company dismisses criticism, since audiences then interpret the response as evidence that the original insensitivity was structural rather than accidental.

PART 3 · 13

Will traditional advertising disappear?

Suggested phrasal verbs
wear offprove worthwhile
Model answer · 127 words

Traditional advertising will probably shrink but not disappear. Public posters, television campaigns and print still provide shared experiences that personalised advertising cannot reproduce. A memorable piece of brand storytelling may reach diverse audiences and become part of public culture. Digital messages are efficient, but market saturation and ad fatigue mean their effect can wear off. Companies will therefore use a mixture of formats. Traditional campaigns may prove worthwhile when a brand needs broad recognition, while targeted promotion will remain useful for specific products and measurable sales. Public campaigns, packaging and event sponsorship will remain useful where brands need broad awareness, while digital promotion handles more individual conversion and measurement. Independent review would show whether the institution or company is solving the stated problem within its lawful mandate.

PART 3 · 14

What responsibility do consumers have for overconsumption?

Suggested phrasal verbs
rein inshop around
Model answer · 125 words

Consumers have responsibility because repeated buying habits create demand and an environmental cost. People can rein in unnecessary replacement, shop around for durable products and resist urgency messages. However, responsibility should not be assigned to individuals alone. consumer autonomy operates within systems designed by companies, platforms and governments. When repair is expensive, advertising is constant and products are intentionally short-lived, individual restraint becomes harder. A fair approach combines personal reflection with product standards, transparent marketing and business models that reward durability rather than rapid disposal. Individual restraint matters, but responsibility must be shared with firms and platforms that design endless exposure, artificial scarcity and disposable product cycles. Independent review would show whether the institution or company is solving the stated problem within its lawful mandate.

PART 3 · 15

What would ethical advertising look like?

Suggested phrasal verbs
accumulate over timeprove worthwhile
Model answer · 125 words

Ethical advertising would still persuade, but it would do so without hiding material information or exploiting vulnerable people. commercial transparency would make sponsorship and data use clear, while claims would be supported by evidence. ethical marketing would also avoid false urgency and allow consumers to reject an offer easily. balanced regulation can establish minimum standards, but companies should go beyond legal compliance because honest communication can accumulate over time long-term trust. In many markets, that approach may prove worthwhile commercially as customers become more sceptical of manipulation and more loyal to firms that treat them fairly. It would also respect a person's decision not to engage, avoiding repeated tracking or interface pressure after a clear refusal. Published evidence would make that judgement easier to defend.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

Some people believe advertising is beneficial because it informs consumers and supports businesses, while others think it manipulates people and encourages unnecessary consumption. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Optional collocation bank
purchase-behaviour dataimpulse buyinghousehold debtdisclosure labelsshopper datamisleading claimscommercial media literacycommercial attention marketalgorithmic targeting
Model essay · 320 words

Advertising is often criticised for encouraging people to buy things they do not need, although it also gives consumers information and helps businesses compete. In my view, advertising is useful in principle, but modern digital promotion requires stronger rules because commercial messages are increasingly personalised and difficult to recognise.

On the positive side, advertising informs the public about new products, prices and services. This is particularly important for smaller companies that need to attract customers away from established brands. Advertising also finances newspapers, online videos and other forms of content that people can access cheaply or free of charge. Some campaigns are creative and memorable, so advertising can contribute to popular culture as well as economic activity.

However, promotion becomes harmful when it exploits insecurity or hides its commercial purpose. Influencers may present paid recommendations as personal advice, while targeted adverts use purchase-behaviour data to reach consumers at vulnerable moments. In addition, scarcity messages and one-click payment systems encourage impulse buying. This can create unnecessary spending and may contribute to household debt, especially among younger consumers who are exposed to advertising throughout the day.

For this reason, governments should require clear disclosure labels, protect shopper data and restrict misleading claims. Advertising aimed at children should face particularly strict standards. At the same time, a complete ban would be unrealistic and would reduce competition. commercial media literacy education can help consumers understand persuasive techniques and compare products more carefully. Smaller firms particularly need affordable access to audiences, but that benefit depends on transparent comparison rather than the purchase of hidden influence. Regulators should therefore preserve truthful promotion while requiring clear sponsorship, complete prices and genuine control over profiling.

In conclusion, advertising provides real benefits by sharing information and supporting business, but it should not be allowed to manipulate people through hidden sponsorship or deceptive design. Transparent advertising should remain legal, while practices that undermine informed choice should be regulated more firmly.

TASK 2 · 2

Advertising aimed at children should be completely banned. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
advertising literacyvulnerable consumersshopper datamisleading claimscommercial attention marketalgorithmic targetingpersonalised advertisingsponsored contentnative advertising
Model essay · 302 words

Children are exposed to advertising through television, games, social media and online video. Although I agree that they require much stronger protection than adults, I do not believe every form of advertising aimed at them should be prohibited.

A strong case for restriction exists because children have limited advertising literacy. Younger audiences may not recognise persuasive intent and can easily associate products with popularity or happiness. Marketing for unhealthy food, gambling-like games or expensive digital purchases can therefore exploit vulnerable consumers and place pressure on parents. Behavioural targeting is particularly problematic because it uses shopper data to personalise messages for people who cannot give meaningful consent.

However, a complete ban would be difficult to define and enforce. Advertisements for books, educational services, cultural events or safe toys may provide useful information. In addition, children frequently encounter media designed for mixed-age audiences, so separating all commercial messages would be unrealistic. A total prohibition could also reduce funding for children’s content without necessarily eliminating exposure.

A more effective approach would combine strict product-based rules with clear disclosure and education. Governments should ban manipulative techniques, restrict advertising for harmful products and prevent personalised tracking of minors. Schools should teach advertising literacy, while platforms should provide parents with practical controls. Companies that make misleading claims should face meaningful penalties. A practical policy should prohibit behavioural profiling, embedded sales inside games and promotion of harmful products, while requiring simple disclosure and frequency limits elsewhere. Independent review must assess what children actually understand at different ages. Educational, cultural and public-health messages should remain possible, as should factual communication to parents, provided commercial material cannot disguise itself as play or friendship.

In conclusion, children deserve firm protection, but a complete ban is unnecessarily broad. Carefully targeted regulation can reduce harm while allowing transparent promotion of appropriate products and services.

TASK 2 · 3

Influencers are replacing traditional advertising as the main way companies promote products. Do the advantages of this development outweigh the disadvantages?

Optional collocation bank
influencer marketingtarget audienceparasocial trustsponsored contentcommercial intentaffiliate linksdisclosure labelspublic trustcommercial attention market
Model essay · 313 words

Influencers have become central to modern promotion because they offer brands direct access to specific online communities. This development has commercial advantages, but I believe the disadvantages outweigh them unless strong disclosure rules are applied.

The main advantage is relevance. Traditional advertising often reaches a broad audience, whereas influencer marketing can connect a product with people who already share an interest in the topic. Smaller companies may reach a target audience without purchasing expensive television or print campaigns. Creators can also demonstrate products in realistic settings, answer questions and explain practical details. These qualities may make promotion more informative and engaging.

Nevertheless, the persuasive power of influencers depends heavily on parasocial trust. Followers may regard a creator as a familiar person rather than as a paid commercial partner. When sponsored content resembles ordinary advice, audiences can misunderstand the commercial intent of the message. affiliate links create a further conflict because the creator benefits directly from sales. Younger users are especially vulnerable to this mixture of entertainment, friendship and marketing.

The solution is not to prohibit influencer advertising, but to require visible disclosure labels and honest claims. Platforms should make paid relationships clear before viewers engage with the content, and influencers should explain when links generate commission. Repeated violations should damage both earnings and account visibility. The balance becomes more favourable when platforms display sponsorship before the recommendation, preserve an accessible advert history and punish repeated concealment. Influencers should substantiate factual health or financial claims and disclose affiliate payment. Audiences also need commercial media literacy, because a label alone cannot neutralise the persuasive force of parasocial trust or a carefully edited lifestyle. Together, these protections slow the decision and make commercial responsibility visible before an impulse becomes a lasting expense.

Overall, influencer marketing can be relevant and creative, but hidden sponsorship weakens public trust. Without effective transparency, its disadvantages are greater than the benefits.

TASK 2 · 4

Companies increasingly use personal data to show consumers personalised advertisements. Is this a positive or negative development?

Optional collocation bank
shopper datapersonalised advertisingpurchase-behaviour dataconsumer profilingsurveillance advertisingdefault settingsconsumer autonomymeaningful consumer consentadvertising-regulation scrutiny
Model essay · 302 words

The use of shopper data has made advertising far more individualised. Although relevant recommendations can be convenient, I consider the overall development negative because the scale of tracking often exceeds what consumers understand or genuinely accept.

personalised advertising has clear practical benefits. It can reduce irrelevant messages and help users discover products connected with their interests. For small businesses, accurate targeting may also reduce marketing costs because advertisements are shown to consumers who are more likely to respond. In principle, this can improve efficiency for both firms and customers.

However, these advantages depend on extensive purchase-behaviour data and consumer profiling. People rarely know how many organisations collect their information, how long it is stored or how it is combined with other records. surveillance advertising can reveal sensitive interests and target individuals when they are vulnerable. Consent is often expressed through complex notices or default settings rather than a meaningful decision. As a result, consumer autonomy is weakened.

Governments should therefore require clear meaningful consumer consent, limit the use of sensitive data and allow users to opt out without losing basic access. advertising-regulation scrutiny is also needed for data brokers, since their role is largely invisible to the public. Less intrusive contextual advertising should remain available as an alternative. A less harmful model would use broad contextual signals, collect less shopper data and separate access to a service from consent to profiling. Users should be able to inspect categories, correct errors and withdraw easily. Sensitive information about health, debt, children or crisis should never determine promotion. These safeguards preserve some relevance without treating every private action as raw material for commercial influence.

In conclusion, relevant advertising is useful, but it does not justify opaque tracking. Until consumers have genuine control over shopper data, personalised advertising should be regarded mainly as a negative development.

TASK 2 · 5

Many people buy products they do not need and later experience financial stress. What causes this problem, and what measures could reduce it?

Optional collocation bank
shopping culturestatus consumptionscarcity tacticssocial proofimpulse buyinghousehold debtcommercial media literacyshopping platformsethical marketing
Model essay · 314 words

Unnecessary purchasing is common in societies where shopping is convenient, emotionally rewarding and constantly promoted. The problem is caused by both psychological pressure and commercial design, so effective solutions must address individuals and the market environment.

One cause is the growth of a shopping culture in which products communicate identity and status. Social media exposes users to carefully selected lifestyles, encouraging status consumption and comparison. At the same time, scarcity tactics, countdown timers and social proof create urgency. Easy credit and one-click payment remove the practical pause that once separated desire from purchase. Repeated impulse buying can then contribute to household debt.

Consumers can reduce the problem by delaying non-essential purchases, setting budgets and comparing alternatives. commercial media literacy education should explain how urgency, influencer promotion and personalised feeds shape behaviour. Banks and payment services could also provide clearer warnings when short-term credit becomes expensive.

Businesses and regulators have responsibilities as well. shopping platforms should display total prices early, avoid misleading scarcity messages and make recurring subscriptions easy to cancel. ethical marketing would still allow persuasion but would not depend on confusion. Governments could restrict dark patterns and require transparent advertising, especially where vulnerable consumers are involved. Governments can require complete prices, easy cancellation and cooling-off periods for high-pressure sales. Banks and platforms can offer voluntary spending alerts without selling the resulting information to advertisers. Schools should teach budgeting alongside commercial media literacy, while firms face consequences for subscription traps and fabricated scarcity. These measures restore time and information at the moment when an impulse becomes a financial commitment. Together, these protections slow the decision and make commercial responsibility visible before an impulse becomes a lasting expense.

In conclusion, financial stress from unnecessary buying is not simply a failure of self-control. It results from social comparison and commercial systems designed to accelerate decisions. Education, better personal habits and fairer digital design are all required.

Course index · Module 1 · Chapter 20
